title: Loading Dataset
text:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import requests
import time

from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor


# List of all individual country codes from the World Bank (ISO 2-letter codes)
# Full list can be found here: https://datahelpdesk.worldbank.org/knowledgebase/articles/906519
country_codes = [
    'AF', 'AL', 'DZ', 'AS', 'AD', 'AO', 'AI', 'AG', 'AR', 'AM', 'AW', 'AU', 'AT', 'AZ', 'BS', 'BH', 'BD', 'BB', 'BY', 'BE', 'BZ', 'BJ', 'BM', 'BT', 'BO', 'BA', 'BW', 'BR', 'BN', 'BG', 'BF', 'BI', 'KH', 'CM', 'CA', 'CV', 'KY', 'CF', 'TD', 'CL', 'CN', 'CO', 'KM', 'CG', 'CD', 'CR', 'CI', 'HR', 'CU', 'CY', 'CZ', 'DK', 'DJ', 'DM', 'DO', 'EC', 'EG', 'SV', 'GQ', 'ER', 'EE', 'ET', 'FO', 'FJ', 'FI', 'FR', 'GA', 'GM', 'GE', 'DE', 'GH', 'GR', 'GD', 'GU', 'GT', 'GN', 'GW', 'GY', 'HT', 'HN', 'HU', 'IS', 'IN', 'ID', 'IR', 'IQ', 'IE', 'IL', 'IT', 'JM', 'JP', 'JO', 'KZ', 'KE', 'KI', 'KP', 'KR', 'KW', 'KG', 'LA', 'LV', 'LB', 'LS', 'LR', 'LY', 'LI', 'LT', 'LU', 'MG', 'MW', 'MY', 'MV', 'ML', 'MT', 'MH', 'MR', 'MU', 'MX', 'FM', 'MD', 'MC', 'MN', 'ME', 'MA', 'MZ', 'MM', 'NA', 'NR', 'NP', 'NL', 'NZ', 'NI', 'NE', 'NG', 'NO', 'OM', 'PK', 'PW', 'PA', 'PG', 'PY', 'PE', 'PH', 'PL', 'PT', 'PR', 'QA', 'RO', 'RU', 'RW', 'WS', 'SM', 'ST', 'SA', 'SN', 'RS', 'SC', 'SL', 'SG', 'SK', 'SI', 'SB', 'SO', 'ZA', 'SS', 'ES', 'LK', 'SD', 'SR', 'SE', 'CH', 'SY', 'TW', 'TJ', 'TZ', 'TH', 'TL', 'TG', 'TO', 'TT', 'TN', 'TR', 'TM', 'TV', 'UG', 'UA', 'AE', 'GB', 'US', 'UY', 'UZ', 'VU', 'VE', 'VN', 'YE', 'ZM', 'ZW'
]

# Define the indicators (based on World Bank codes)
indicators = {
    
    'GDP Growth (%)': 'NY.GDP.MKTP.KD.ZG',
    'FDI (% of GDP)': 'BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS',
    'Inflation (%)': 'FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG',
    'Unemployment (%)': 'SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS',
    'Trade Balance (% of GDP)': 'NE.RSB.GNFS.ZS'
}

# Define parameters
start_year = 2000
end_year = 2023
base_url = "https://api.worldbank.org/v2/country/{}/indicator/{}?date={}:{}&format=json&per_page=1000"


# Reduce batch size and split by indicator
batch_size = 5

# Storage for fetched data
data = []

# Loop over indicators one at a time
for name, code in indicators.items():
    print(f"Fetching data for: {name}")
    
    for i in range(0, len(country_codes), batch_size):
        batch = country_codes[i:i + batch_size]
        countries = ";".join(batch)
        url = base_url.format(countries, code, start_year, end_year)
        
        response = requests.get(url)
        if response.status_code == 200:
            json_data = response.json()
            if len(json_data) > 1:
                for entry in json_data[1]:
                    data.append({
                        'Country': entry['country']['value'],
                        'Year': entry['date'],
                        'Indicator': name,
                        'Value': entry.get('value', None)
                    })
        else:
            print(f"Failed to fetch {name} for countries: {countries} (Status code: {response.status_code})")
        
        # Avoid hitting request limit
        time.sleep(0.2)

# Convert to DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

Now we load the other dataset called WGI dataset which we downloaded directly from the website (without API). This dataset contains political stability and corruption control score of countries throughout years. Due to mismatches of country names on the 2 datasets, we will rename some of the country name values in the WGI dataset.

# Load the WGI dataset
wgi = pd.read_excel('data//wgidataset.xlsx')

# Keep only the necessary columns
wgi = wgi[['code', 'countryname', 'year', 'indicator', 'estimate']]

# Create a mapping for mismatched country names
country_mapping = {
    "Côte d'Ivoire": "Cote d'Ivoire",
     "Cape Verde":"Cabo Verde",
     "Czech Republic":"Czechia",
    "Türkiye":"Turkiye" 
}


# Reshape to wide format (optional)
df_pivot = df.pivot(index=['Country', 'Year'], columns='Indicator', values='Value').reset_index()

Data Cleaning and Imputation
Strategy: 
Remove countries with:  - Any missing values in GDP growth. - Any missing values in FDI (% of GDP).
Imputation:  - Linear interpolation → Time-based data (inflation, trade balance). - Forward/backward fill → Data gaps within the same country. - Mean/median → Non-time-based data (e.g., unemployment). - KNN → Final refinement if gaps remain after other methods.

# Identify countries with any missing GDP Growth or FDI values
countries_to_drop = df_pivot.groupby('Country').filter(
    lambda x: x['GDP Growth (%)'].isna().any() or x['FDI (% of GDP)'].isna().any()
)['Country'].unique()

# Drop the entire country from the dataset
df_pivot = df_pivot[~df_pivot['Country'].isin(countries_to_drop)]

We drop countries with more than 30% missing data in each column. The remaining missing values are imputted using Forward and backward fill for inflation and trade balance, while we use linear interpolation for unemployment (rest index to match original).

# Step 1: Drop countries with more than 30% missing data
missing_threshold = 0.30 * 24  # 30% of 24 years ≈ 7 missing values

# Identify countries exceeding the threshold
countries_to_drop = df_pivot.groupby('Country').apply(
    lambda x: x.isna().sum().max() > missing_threshold
)

# Drop those countries
df_pivot = df_pivot[~df_pivot['Country'].isin(countries_to_drop[countries_to_drop].index)]

# Step 2: Impute the remaining missing values

# Forward and backward fill for inflation and trade balance
df_pivot['Inflation (%)'] = df_pivot.groupby('Country')['Inflation (%)'].ffill().bfill()
df_pivot['Trade Balance (% of GDP)'] = df_pivot.groupby('Country')['Trade Balance (% of GDP)'].ffill().bfill()

# Linear interpolation for unemployment (reset index to match original)
df_pivot['Unemployment (%)'] = (
    df_pivot.groupby('Country')['Unemployment (%)']
    .apply(lambda x: x.interpolate())
    .reset_index(level=0, drop=True)
)

# Step 3: Confirm that no missing values remain
print(df_pivot.isna().sum())

missing_2001_corruption = df_merged.loc[df_merged['Year'] == 2001, 'corruption_score'].isna().sum()

Missing corruption data for 2001: 134
Missing political stability data for 2001: 134


By the above result, we observed that the WGI with which we just merged our pivot data does not provide us with data for corruption_score and political_stability_score in year 2001. This is why we have 123 missing values in both columns which is from each of the 123 countries in our dataset.
Imputation
Notice that each country in WGI dataset does not have observation for year 2001. Therefore, we need to perform some imputation.

Before interpolating, let’s see how smooth or erratic the trends are over time.
If the trends are smooth, interpolation makes sense.
If there are jumps or spikes, interpolation might distort the data.


sample_countries = ['United States', 'Indonesia', 'Germany', 'Brazil']

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
for country in sample_countries:
    data = df_merged[df_merged['Country'] == country]
    sns.lineplot(x=data['Year'], y=data['corruption_score'], label=country)
    
plt.title('Corruption Score Trends (Sample Countries)')
plt.legend()
plt.show()









plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
for country in sample_countries:
    data = df_merged[df_merged['Country'] == country]
    sns.lineplot(x=data['Year'], y=data['political_stability_score'], label=country)
    
plt.title('Political Stability Score Trends (Sample Countries)')
plt.legend()
plt.show()








Since the the trends are too volatile and noisy for simple linear interpolation to make sense, we will do forward/backward fill instead. Governance scores tend to be stable over short periods — political structures don’t change drastically year-to-year. Forward/backward fill smooths out small gaps without distorting long-term patterns.

# Forward fill first, then backward fill (by country)
df_merged['corruption_score'] = df_merged.groupby('Country')['corruption_score'].ffill().bfill()
df_merged['political_stability_score'] = df_merged.groupby('Country')['political_stability_score'].ffill().bfill()

Handling outliers
From the pairplot, we can already see some extreme outliers:

FDI (% of GDP) → Some countries have very large FDI percentages (over 200%) — probably financial hubs or tax havens.
GDP Growth (%) → A few points showing growth rates beyond ±50% — likely data entry issues or extraordinary events (e.g., hyperinflation or post-crisis rebounds).
Inflation (%) → Some very high spikes — possibly reflecting hyperinflation in unstable economies.

We’ll clip values at the 1st percentile and 99th percentile to avoid extreme values distorting the model

# Clip extreme outliers for FDI, GDP growth, inflation, trade balance, and unemployment
for col in ['FDI (% of GDP)', 'GDP Growth (%)', 'Inflation (%)', 
            'Trade Balance (% of GDP)', 'Unemployment (%)']:
    lower_bound = df_merged[col].quantile(0.01)
    upper_bound = df_merged[col].quantile(0.99)
    df_merged[col] = np.clip(df_merged[col], lower_bound, upper_bound)

Summary Statistics Analysis

1. FDI (% of GDP)

Mean = 4.37% – On average, FDI represents a significant part of GDP.

High standard deviation (7.19%) – Large variability across countries and time periods.

Positive skewness (4.18) – Strong right skew, indicating that some countries attract significantly more FDI than others.

High kurtosis (22.20) – Heavy tails, suggesting rare but extreme FDI spikes (e.g., large capital inflows to certain economies).
Insight: The distribution of FDI is highly uneven, which may require addressing outliers during modeling.




2. GDP Growth (%)

Mean = 3.58% – Moderate growth rate on average.

Negative skewness (-0.48) – Slight left skew, suggesting more frequent negative growth periods than extreme positive growth.

Low kurtosis (1.67) – Relatively normal distribution, with no heavy tails.
Insight: GDP growth shows modest variation, implying that most countries experience small growth fluctuations rather than large swings.




3. Inflation (%)

Mean = 5.13% – Moderate inflation rate across the sample.

Positive skewness (2.88) – Indicates that some countries experience extreme inflation spikes (e.g., hyperinflation).

High kurtosis (11.10) – Strong outliers, reflecting occasional inflation crises.
Insight: High kurtosis and skewness suggest that inflation crises may need to be treated as special cases in the model.




4. Trade Balance (% of GDP)

Mean = -2.80% – Most countries have a trade deficit.

Large spread (std = 12.70%) – Suggests trade balance is highly volatile.

Low skewness (-0.09) – Almost symmetric distribution.

Low kurtosis (1.71) – Consistent distribution with no major outliers.
Insight: Trade balance behaves more stably than inflation or FDI, but deficits dominate the overall trend.




5. Unemployment (%)

Mean = 7.43% – Moderate global unemployment rate.

Positive skewness (1.26) – Indicates that some countries face structural unemployment issues.

Low kurtosis (1.20) – Relatively consistent distribution.
Insight: Unemployment has a moderate spread, but not many extreme cases, which suggests relatively stable labor market trends.




6. Corruption Score

Mean = 0.08 – On average, countries have weak-to-moderate control of corruption.

Positive skewness (0.66) – More countries have lower corruption control than higher.

Negative kurtosis (-0.55) – Lighter tails, indicating fewer extreme cases of corruption control.
Insight: Corruption control varies moderately across countries, indicating potential for non-linear effects in the model.




7. Political Stability Score

Mean = -0.001 – Political stability hovers around neutral levels on average.

Negative skewness (-0.43) – More countries experience mild instability than strong stability.

Negative kurtosis (-0.34) – Less variation and fewer extreme cases of political stability.
Insight: Political stability is less volatile than corruption, suggesting more consistent long-term trends.




Takeaways for the Model

FDI, Inflation, and Corruption – Show heavy tails, which could make tree-based models (like XGBoost) more effective than linear models.

GDP Growth and Political Stability – More balanced distributions, suggesting they could perform well with linear models like panel regression.

Trade Balance and Unemployment – Moderate spread and skew, which should perform consistently across different models.

High kurtosis in FDI and Inflation – May require testing the effect of outliers on model performance through clipping or transformation.

import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.api as sm

# Copy your main DataFrame
df_fe = df_merged.copy()

# --- STEP 1: One-hot encode the categorical levels (drop first to avoid multicollinearity)
level_dummies = pd.get_dummies(df_fe[['corruption_level', 'political_stability_level']], 
                                drop_first=True).astype(int)

# --- STEP 2: Create interaction terms manually
# We multiply FDI with each dummy column to form interactions
for col in level_dummies.columns:
    df_fe[f'FDI_x_{col}'] = df_fe['FDI (% of GDP)'] * level_dummies[col]

# --- STEP 3: Select core numeric variables
X_core = df_fe[['FDI (% of GDP)', 'Inflation (%)', 'Unemployment (%)', 'Trade Balance (% of GDP)']]

# --- STEP 4: Create country dummies
country_dummies = pd.get_dummies(df_fe['Country'], drop_first=True).astype(int)

# --- STEP 5: Combine all predictors
# Use level dummies and interaction terms
interaction_terms = df_fe.filter(like='FDI_x_')
X_panel = pd.concat([X_core, level_dummies, interaction_terms, country_dummies], axis=1)

# --- STEP 6: Ensure all are numeric
X_panel = X_panel.apply(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce')
y_panel = pd.to_numeric(df_fe['GDP Growth (%)'], errors='coerce')

# --- STEP 7: Drop rows with any missing values
panel_data = pd.concat([y_panel, X_panel], axis=1).dropna()
y_clean = panel_data.iloc[:, 0]
X_clean = sm.add_constant(panel_data.iloc[:, 1:])

# --- STEP 8: Fit the OLS model
panel_model = sm.OLS(y_clean, X_clean)
panel_results = panel_model.fit()

# --- STEP 9: Output results
print(panel_results.summary())

# Copy your main DataFrame
df_fe = df_merged.copy()

# --- Step 1: Create interaction terms (continuous version) ---
df_fe['FDI_x_Corruption'] = df_fe['FDI (% of GDP)'] * df_fe['corruption_score']
df_fe['FDI_x_Political'] = df_fe['FDI (% of GDP)'] * df_fe['political_stability_score']

# --- Step 2: Create country dummies (drop one to avoid multicollinearity) ---
country_dummies = pd.get_dummies(df_fe['Country'], drop_first=True).astype(int)

# --- Step 3: Select core independent variables ---
X_core = df_fe[['FDI (% of GDP)', 'corruption_score', 'political_stability_score',
                'FDI_x_Corruption', 'FDI_x_Political', 'Inflation (%)',
                'Unemployment (%)', 'Trade Balance (% of GDP)']]

# --- Step 4: Combine predictors ---
X_panel = pd.concat([X_core, country_dummies], axis=1)

# --- Step 5: Ensure all columns are numeric ---
X_panel = X_panel.apply(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce')
y_panel = pd.to_numeric(df_fe['GDP Growth (%)'], errors='coerce')

# --- Step 6: Drop rows with missing values ---
panel_data = pd.concat([y_panel, X_panel], axis=1).dropna()
y_clean = panel_data.iloc[:, 0]
X_clean = sm.add_constant(panel_data.iloc[:, 1:])

# --- Step 7: Fit the OLS model ---
panel_model = sm.OLS(y_clean, X_clean)
panel_results = panel_model.fit()

# --- Step 8: Output results ---
print(panel_results.summary())

# --- Step 1: Create SHAP explainer ---

# --- Step 2: Summary plot (overall importance) ---
shap.summary_plot(shap_values, X, plot_type="bar")

# --- Step 3: Summary plot (per feature contribution) ---
shap.summary_plot(shap_values, X)

# --- Step 4: Interaction plots ---
# FDI × Political Stability
shap.plots.scatter(
    shap_values[:, X.columns.get_loc('FDI (% of GDP)')],
    color=shap_values[:, X.columns.get_loc('political_stability_score')],
    title="SHAP Interaction: FDI vs Political Stability"
)

# Optional: FDI × Corruption
shap.plots.scatter(
    shap_values[:, X.columns.get_loc('FDI (% of GDP)')],
    color=shap_values[:, X.columns.get_loc('corruption_score')],
    title="SHAP Interaction: FDI vs Corruption"
)































FDI * Political stability
This SHAP interaction plot shows that FDI tends to have a stronger positive impact on GDP growth in countries with lower political stability. As FDI increases, its effect on GDP growth (measured by SHAP value) rises more sharply when political stability is low (blue) compared to when it’s high (red).
This supports the idea that FDI may be more impactful in politically fragile contexts, possibly by compensating for institutional weaknesses or lack of domestic investment.


FDI * Corruption
While FDI generally increases predicted GDP growth across all levels of corruption, this SHAP interaction plot suggests that its effect is somewhat stronger in countries with lower corruption scores. In contrast, the benefit of FDI appears slightly diminished in more corrupt environments.
However, unlike the case with political stability, this interaction is weaker and more scattered, indicating that corruption may play a lesser or more nonlinear moderating role in FDI’s impact on growth.

]

# Define the indicators (based on World Bank codes)
indicators = {
    
    'GDP Growth (%)': 'NY.GDP.MKTP.KD.ZG',
    'FDI (% of GDP)': 'BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS',
    'Inflation (%)': 'FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG',
    'Unemployment (%)': 'SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS',
    'Trade Balance (% of GDP)': 'NE.RSB.GNFS.ZS'
}

# Define parameters
start_year = 2000
end_year = 2023
base_url = "https://api.worldbank.org/v2/country/{}/indicator/{}?date={}:{}&format=json&per_page=1000"
# Reduce batch size and split by indicator
batch_size = 5

# Storage for fetched data
data = []

# Loop over indicators one at a time
for name, code in indicators.items():
    print(f"Fetching data for: {name}")
    
    for i in range(0, len(country_codes), batch_size):
        batch = country_codes[i:i + batch_size]
        countries = ";".join(batch)
        url = base_url.format(countries, code, start_year, end_year)
        
        response = requests.get(url)
        if response.status_code == 200:
            json_data = response.json()
            if len(json_data) > 1:
                for entry in json_data[1]:
                    data.append({
                        'Country': entry['country']['value'],
                        'Year': entry['date'],
                        'Indicator': name,
                        'Value': entry.get('value', None)
                    })
        else:
            print(f"Failed to fetch {name} for countries: {countries} (Status code: {response.status_code})")
        
        # Avoid hitting request limit
        time.sleep(0.2)

# Convert to DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

title: Loading Dataset
text:
Interactive Plots
SHAP Summary Plot

import shap
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

# Step 1: Calculate SHAP values
explainer = shap.Explainer(chosen_xgboost)
shap_values = explainer(X)

# Step 2: Prepare data for Plotly
mean_abs_shap = np.abs(shap_values.values).mean(axis=0)
shap_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Feature': X.columns,
    'Mean |SHAP value|': mean_abs_shap
}).sort_values(by='Mean |SHAP value|', ascending=True)

# Step 3: Create interactive bar chart
fig = px.bar(
    shap_df,
    x='Mean |SHAP value|',
    y='Feature',
    orientation='h',
    title='SHAP Summary Plot (Interactive)',
    height=500
)

# Step 4: Save as HTML for Quarto embedding
fig.write_html("figures/shap_summary.html", include_plotlyjs='cdn')

SHAP interaction plots

import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd

# Get raw values
fdi_values = X['FDI (% of GDP)']
shap_fdi = shap_values[:, X.columns.get_loc('FDI (% of GDP)')].values
political_stability = X['political_stability_score']

# Combine into a DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'FDI (% of GDP)': fdi_values,
    'SHAP value for FDI': shap_fdi,
    'Political Stability': political_stability
})

# Create Plotly plot
fig = px.scatter(
    df,
    x='FDI (% of GDP)',
    y='SHAP value for FDI',
    color='Political Stability',
    color_continuous_scale='RdBu',
    title='SHAP Interaction: FDI vs Political Stability',
    height=500
)

fig.write_html("figures/shap_interaction_stability.html", include_plotlyjs='cdn')


df2 = pd.DataFrame({
    'FDI (% of GDP)': fdi_values,
    'SHAP value for FDI': shap_fdi,
    'Corruption': X['corruption_score']
})

fig2 = px.scatter(
    df2,
    x='FDI (% of GDP)',
    y='SHAP value for FDI',
    color='Corruption',
    color_continuous_scale='RdBu',
    title='SHAP Interaction: FDI vs Corruption',
    height=500
)

fig2.write_html("figures/shap_interaction_corruption.html", include_plotlyjs='cdn')

title: About
text:
This project explores how the impact of Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) on GDP growth varies depending on a country’s political stability and corruption levels. Using panel data from 2000 to 2023 across multiple countries, we combine fixed-effects regression and machine learning (XGBoost + SHAP) to uncover interaction effects between FDI and governance quality.
Key findings suggest that FDI has a stronger growth-enhancing effect in politically unstable environments, while the moderating role of corruption is less consistent. The project highlights how governance context influences FDI effectiveness and offers actionable insights for policymakers and international investors seeking to foster sustainable economic development.

title: ML Analysis
text:
To complement our statistical analysis, we applied XGBoost (eXtreme Gradient Boosting), a tree-based ensemble method well-suited for tabular data with complex, nonlinear interactions.
Unlike the panel regression model, which assumes linear relationships and focuses on statistical significance, XGBoost allowed us to uncover nonlinear effects and interactions between FDI and governance indicators that may not be easily captured through regression alone.

title: ML Analysis
text:
To complement our statistical analysis, we applied XGBoost (eXtreme Gradient Boosting), a tree-based ensemble method well-suited for tabular data with complex, nonlinear interactions.
Unlike the panel regression model, which assumes linear relationships and focuses on statistical significance, XGBoost allowed us to uncover nonlinear effects and interactions between FDI and governance indicators that may not be easily captured through regression alone.

title: ML Analysis
text:
Feature Importance and SHAP
We used SHAP (SHapley Additive Explanations) to interpret the XGBoost model. SHAP provides feature-level explanations by estimating how much each variable contributes to the model’s prediction for GDP growth.

FDI (% of GDP) was consistently the most influential feature, confirming its strong direct relationship with growth.
Unemployment and trade balance were also important predictors, consistent with macroeconomic expectations.
Governance scores — political stability and corruption — had weaker direct effects but contributed meaningfully via interactions.

title: ML Analysis
text:
Interaction Effects
To understand how governance moderates the impact of FDI, we analyzed SHAP interaction plots.

FDI × Political Stability

In countries with low political stability, FDI had a stronger positive effect on GDP growth.
As political stability increased, the marginal effect of FDI diminished.
This suggests that in fragile environments, FDI may compensate for weak domestic investment or governance gaps.





FDI × Corruption

The interaction between FDI and corruption was weaker and less consistent.
There was a modest trend suggesting that FDI is slightly more effective in less corrupt countries, but the pattern was diffuse and statistically insignificant.

title: ML Analysis
text:
Key Insight

Political stability significantly moderates the FDI–growth relationship, with FDI being most effective in politically unstable environments — a finding consistent across both econometric and machine learning models.

In contrast, corruption does not show a clear moderating effect in how FDI translates into economic growth, though it may influence FDI inflows themselves — a topic for further study.

title: ML Analysis
text:
Limitations

The XGBoost model was trained for interpretation, not prediction, so performance metrics (e.g., R² ≈ 0.40) should be viewed in context.
SHAP interpretations reveal patterns, but they remain correlational rather than causal.
No cross-validation was used, as the goal was stable feature rankings and explainability.
